CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): Overall the paper is well organized and readable: it follows a clear intro→method→experiments→conclusion flow and states the main contributions early (first full-coverage MRS parser, stack-based features, joint prediction). Notation and methodology appear consistent and well defined, hyperparameters are reported, and related work is covered thoroughly, which aids reproducibility. Figures, tables, and examples are described as effective and the writing quality is good, but a few presentation points could be clarified (e.g., the practical implications of the top‑down linearization’s lossy reentrancy recovery and the evaluation exclusion of ~15% of sentences) to make limitations and heuristics more transparent. | B(4): The paper is overall well organized and the main contributions are stated early (first full-coverage neural MRS parser, transition-based encoder-decoder design, stack features and joint prediction). Notation and methodology are presented clearly enough to follow the encoder-decoder, arc-eager/top-down linearizations, and attention variants, and experiments use appropriate metrics (EDM, Smatch, predicate/argument F1) and datasets (DeepBank, LDC AMR). Figures/tables and examples are reported as effective and related work coverage is thorough, but some design choices (e.g. potential lossiness of the top-down linearization and heuristic recovery of reentrancies) could use clearer exposition to remove minor ambiguity for readers focused on representation trade-offs.

Notation and methodology are presented clearly enough to follow the encoder-decoder, arc-eager/top-down linearizations, and attention variants, and experiments use appropriate metrics (EDM, Smatch, predicate/argument F1) and datasets (DeepBank, LDC AMR). Figures/tables and examples are reported as effective and related work coverage is thorough, but some design choices (e.g. potential lossiness of the top-down linearization and heuristic recovery of reentrancies) could use clearer exposition to remove minor ambiguity for readers focused on representation trade-offs.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): This paper tackles an important problem—bringing neural parsing to full-coverage MRS-style semantic graphs—and provides convincing empirical gains (more accurate than attention baselines on MRS and stronger than existing neural AMR baselines, with an 86.69% Smatch). The demonstrated order-of-magnitude speedup via GPU batch processing and the transition-based design that jointly predicts predicates and alignments make the representation more practical and are likely to change how researchers approach semantic graph parsing. Remaining issues (weaker AMR concept prediction, evaluation limited to ERG-acceptable sentences, and no mention of released code) temper transformative potential but do not negate substantial impact and follow-on value for the community. | B(4): This paper addresses an important practical gap by delivering the first full-coverage neural semantic graph parser for MRS, demonstrating strong empirical gains (86.69% Smatch on MRS and improvements over neural AMR baselines) and much faster throughput than a high-precision grammar-based ERG parser. Its joint prediction of predicates and alignments, stack-based features, and transition-based approach materially change what is feasible for broad-coverage semantic parsing and will influence parser design and engineering choices. Limitations in concept prediction for AMR and evaluation restricted to ERG-acceptable sentences moderate its transformative potential, but the work nonetheless opens significant follow-up directions for semantic representation learning and efficient parser deployment.

reviewer_a.justification: This paper tackles an important problem—bringing neural parsing to full-coverage MRS-style semantic graphs—and provides convincing empirical gains (more accurate than attention baselines on MRS and stronger than existing neural AMR baselines, with an 86.69% Smatch). The demonstrated order-of-magnitude speedup via GPU batch processing and the transition-based design that jointly predicts predicates and alignments make the representation more practical and are likely to change how researchers approach semantic graph parsing. Remaining issues (weaker AMR concept prediction, evaluation limited to ERG-acceptable sentences, and no mention of released code) temper transformative potential but do not negate substantial impact and follow-on value for the community.

Its joint prediction of predicates and alignments, stack-based features, and transition-based approach materially change what is feasible for broad-coverage semantic parsing and will influence parser design and engineering choices. Limitations in concept prediction for AMR and evaluation restricted to ERG-acceptable sentences moderate its transformative potential, but the work nonetheless opens significant follow-up directions for semantic representation learning and efficient parser deployment.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): This paper addresses a core NLP/ML problem (neural semantic graph parsing) with methods, datasets (DeepBank/AMR), metrics (Smatch/EDM/F1), and strong baselines that match top-tier conference expectations. Experimental protocol reports hyperparameters and compares to relevant prior work, but reproducibility is a mild concern (code not mentioned) and the evaluation excludes ~15% of sentences due to ERG coverage which should be highlighted for readers. Overall the work is well targeted to the venue’s audience and standards, with only minor scope/reproducibility caveats. | B(5): This paper sits squarely within the scope of a top-tier NLP/ML conference: it addresses semantic graph parsing (MRS/DMRS/EDS and AMR), proposes a neural transition-based parser, and evaluates on standard corpora (DeepBank v1.1 and LDC2015E86) using accepted metrics (EDM, Smatch, predicate/argument F1). The work targets the right audience by advancing both modeling (stack features, pointer-based alignment, joint predicate/alignment prediction) and empirical benchmarks (outperforming neural AMR baselines and offering large speedups vs. a high-precision grammar parser). Methodology, baselines, and evaluation are appropriate for the venue, and the paper is well positioned within the semantic parsing community given its claimed full-coverage MRS contribution and thorough related work coverage.

reviewer_a.justification: This paper addresses a core NLP/ML problem (neural semantic graph parsing) with methods, datasets (DeepBank/AMR), metrics (Smatch/EDM/F1), and strong baselines that match top-tier conference expectations. Experimental protocol reports hyperparameters and compares to relevant prior work, but reproducibility is a mild concern (code not mentioned) and the evaluation excludes ~15% of sentences due to ERG coverage which should be highlighted for readers. Overall the work is well targeted to the venue’s audience and standards, with only minor scope/reproducibility caveats.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper sits squarely within the scope of a top-tier NLP/ML conference: it addresses semantic graph parsing (MRS/DMRS/EDS and AMR), proposes a neural transition-based parser, and evaluates on standard corpora (DeepBank v1.1 and LDC2015E86) using accepted metrics (EDM, Smatch, predicate/argument F1). The work targets the right audience by advancing both modeling (stack features, pointer-based alignment, joint predicate/alignment prediction) and empirical benchmarks (outperforming neural AMR baselines and offering large speedups vs. a high-precision grammar parser). Methodology, baselines, and evaluation are appropriate for the venue, and the paper is well positioned within the semantic parsing community given its claimed full-coverage MRS contribution and thorough related work coverage.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper clearly states its main claims and supports them with experiments on DeepBank and AMR (EDM, Smatch, predicate/argument F1) comparing to appropriate baselines (JAMR, CAMR, seq2seq AMR parsers and a grammar-based ERG parser), and reports hyperparameters—supporting reproducibility. There are no apparent mathematical derivations to check and the evaluation protocol and baselines look sound, but I note methodological caveats the authors themselves report: concept prediction weaknesses on AMR, evaluation restricted to sentences with ERG analyses (~15% excluded), and a potentially lossy top-down linearization for reentrancies that relies on heuristics. These limitations and the absence of an explicit code release modestly weaken reproducibility and leave some claims (e.g., full-coverage MRS parsing) slightly qualified, so I judge the work technically strong but not flawless. | B(4): The paper's primary claims are supported by experimental evidence comparing to sensible baselines (JAMR, CAMR, seq2seq AMR parsers) on standard datasets (DeepBank v1.1, LDC2015E86) with appropriate metrics (EDM, Smatch, predicate/argument F1), and runtime/throughput improvements are plausibly demonstrated via GPU batching. There are no nontrivial mathematical proofs to check, and the experimental methodology appears sound overall, but important caveats remain: ~15% of sentences are excluded due to ERG coverage and concept prediction for AMR is acknowledged as a weakness, which weakens the claim of full-coverage and of uniformly strong concept performance. Given these limitations and the reliance on heuristic recovery for reentrancies in the top-down linearization, I find the paper technically solid but with minor issues that should be better quantified, so I award a 4.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper clearly states its main claims and supports them with experiments on DeepBank and AMR (EDM, Smatch, predicate/argument F1) comparing to appropriate baselines (JAMR, CAMR, seq2seq AMR parsers and a grammar-based ERG parser), and reports hyperparameters—supporting reproducibility. There are no apparent mathematical derivations to check and the evaluation protocol and baselines look sound, but I note methodological caveats the authors themselves report: concept prediction weaknesses on AMR, evaluation restricted to sentences with ERG analyses (~15% excluded), and a potentially lossy top-down linearization for reentrancies that relies on heuristics. These limitations and the absence of an explicit code release modestly weaken reproducibility and leave some claims (e.g., full-coverage MRS parsing) slightly qualified, so I judge the work technically strong but not flawless.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper's primary claims are supported by experimental evidence comparing to sensible baselines (JAMR, CAMR, seq2seq AMR parsers) on standard datasets (DeepBank v1.1, LDC2015E86) with appropriate metrics (EDM, Smatch, predicate/argument F1), and runtime/throughput improvements are plausibly demonstrated via GPU batching. There are no nontrivial mathematical proofs to check, and the experimental methodology appears sound overall, but important caveats remain: ~15% of sentences are excluded due to ERG coverage and concept prediction for AMR is acknowledged as a weakness, which weakens the claim of full-coverage and of uniformly strong concept performance. Given these limitations and the reliance on heuristic recovery for reentrancies in the top-down linearization, I find the paper technically solid but with minor issues that should be better quantified, so I award a 4.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper compares to a strong set of prior systems (JAMR, CAMR, several seq2seq AMR parsers, and a high-precision grammar-based ERG parser) and evaluates on standard datasets (DeepBank/ERG and LDC2015E86) using appropriate metrics (Smatch, EDM, predicate/argument F1). Comparisons appear fair in metric and dataset choice, though evaluation on ERG-derived sentences excludes ~15% of the corpus which may bias speed/accuracy claims. The authors acknowledge when prior methods are competitive and discuss limitations (e.g., concept prediction and lossy top-down linearization), and related work coverage is thorough, so overall the empirical comparison is strong with only minor caveats about evaluation coverage and reproducibility details (code not mentioned). | B(5): The paper compares against a wide set of appropriate and recent baselines for both MRS/DMRS-style parsing and AMR (JAMR, CAMR, seq2seq AMR parsers, hyperedge replacement, and a high-precision grammar-based parser) using standard datasets (DeepBank v1.1, LDC2015E86) and metrics (EDM, Smatch, predicate/argument F1). The authors are transparent about limitations (e.g., concept prediction weakness for AMR, ERG-coverage restrictions) and analyze differences in modeling choices (stack-based features, attention vs. pointer alignment, linearization trade-offs), and the related-work coverage is reported as thorough, supporting a comprehensive and fair empirical comparison.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares to a strong set of prior systems (JAMR, CAMR, several seq2seq AMR parsers, and a high-precision grammar-based ERG parser) and evaluates on standard datasets (DeepBank/ERG and LDC2015E86) using appropriate metrics (Smatch, EDM, predicate/argument F1). Comparisons appear fair in metric and dataset choice, though evaluation on ERG-derived sentences excludes ~15% of the corpus which may bias speed/accuracy claims. The authors acknowledge when prior methods are competitive and discuss limitations (e.g., concept prediction and lossy top-down linearization), and related work coverage is thorough, so overall the empirical comparison is strong with only minor caveats about evaluation coverage and reproducibility details (code not mentioned).

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares against a wide set of appropriate and recent baselines for both MRS/DMRS-style parsing and AMR (JAMR, CAMR, seq2seq AMR parsers, hyperedge replacement, and a high-precision grammar-based parser) using standard datasets (DeepBank v1.1, LDC2015E86) and metrics (EDM, Smatch, predicate/argument F1). The authors are transparent about limitations (e.g., concept prediction weakness for AMR, ERG-coverage restrictions) and analyze differences in modeling choices (stack-based features, attention vs. pointer alignment, linearization trade-offs), and the related-work coverage is reported as thorough, supporting a comprehensive and fair empirical comparison.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper presents a notable novel combination: the first full-coverage neural semantic graph parser for MRS and a transition-based encoder–decoder that integrates stack-based embeddings with pointer-network hard attention for joint predicate and alignment prediction. These elements are clearly distinguished from prior AMR and grammar-based approaches (e.g., JAMR, CAMR, ERG) and the reported joint prediction and incremental transition strategy go beyond standard seq2seq or attention-only baselines. While the techniques build on existing modules (transition parsing, pointer networks), their adaptation to full-coverage MRS parsing and the joint alignment–predicate treatment represent a non-trivial and informative advance for experts in semantic parsing. | B(4): The paper presents a notable and concrete novelty: a transition-based neural parser claimed to be the first full-coverage semantic graph parser for MRS, combining stack-based embedding features with joint prediction of unlexicalized predicates and token alignments and pointer-network hard attention. These elements constitute a non-trivial methodological advance beyond prior AMR/graph parsers and are clearly differentiated from grammar-based and attention-only baselines, with empirical gains and large speed improvements. While not a paradigm shift (some components reuse established neural decoding and pointer ideas) the integrated treatment and demonstration on MRS/AMR represent meaningful new work that would inform experts in semantic parsing.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents a notable novel combination: the first full-coverage neural semantic graph parser for MRS and a transition-based encoder–decoder that integrates stack-based embeddings with pointer-network hard attention for joint predicate and alignment prediction. These elements are clearly distinguished from prior AMR and grammar-based approaches (e.g., JAMR, CAMR, ERG) and the reported joint prediction and incremental transition strategy go beyond standard seq2seq or attention-only baselines. While the techniques build on existing modules (transition parsing, pointer networks), their adaptation to full-coverage MRS parsing and the joint alignment–predicate treatment represent a non-trivial and informative advance for experts in semantic parsing.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper presents a notable and concrete novelty: a transition-based neural parser claimed to be the first full-coverage semantic graph parser for MRS, combining stack-based embedding features with joint prediction of unlexicalized predicates and token alignments and pointer-network hard attention. These elements constitute a non-trivial methodological advance beyond prior AMR/graph parsers and are clearly differentiated from grammar-based and attention-only baselines, with empirical gains and large speed improvements. While not a paradigm shift (some components reuse established neural decoding and pointer ideas) the integrated treatment and demonstration on MRS/AMR represent meaningful new work that would inform experts in semantic parsing.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The experiments are solid: comparisons to numerous strong baselines (JAMR, CAMR, seq2seq AMR parsers) and evaluation on DeepBank and LDC AMR with relevant metrics (EDM, Smatch, predicate/argument F1) support the main claims. The paper reports hyperparameters and includes component analyses (e.g. stack features, hard vs soft attention) and runtime speedups, and it candidly discusses limitations (concept prediction weakness, evaluation restricted to ERG-acceptable sentences, top-down reentrancy heuristics). Missing for full reproducibility are an explicit code/model release and reporting of multiple runs/variance or statistical significance for results; these, plus the 15% data exclusion, are the main gaps preventing a top score. | B(4): The experimental evaluation is strong in breadth: comparisons to multiple AMR baselines and a high-precision grammar-based ERG parser, use of relevant metrics (EDM, Smatch, predicate/argument F1), and a runtime speed analysis that supports the efficiency claim. The paper reports hyperparameters and examines attention variants (hard pointer vs soft attention), but the summary does not clearly indicate a systematic ablation suite isolating all major design choices (e.g., stack features, joint predicate/alignment prediction), so sensitivity analysis is only partial. Related work coverage is reported as thorough, and several concrete limitations are acknowledged (concept prediction weakness for AMR, evaluation restricted to ERG-analysable sentences, potential lossy top-down linearization). Overall the evidence is substantial and well-documented but falls short of exhaustive ablations and full reproducibility (code not mentioned), so a solid but not perfect score is warranted.

reviewer_a.justification: The experiments are solid: comparisons to numerous strong baselines (JAMR, CAMR, seq2seq AMR parsers) and evaluation on DeepBank and LDC AMR with relevant metrics (EDM, Smatch, predicate/argument F1) support the main claims. The paper reports hyperparameters and includes component analyses (e.g. stack features, hard vs soft attention) and runtime speedups, and it candidly discusses limitations (concept prediction weakness, evaluation restricted to ERG-acceptable sentences, top-down reentrancy heuristics). Missing for full reproducibility are an explicit code/model release and reporting of multiple runs/variance or statistical significance for results; these, plus the 15% data exclusion, are the main gaps preventing a top score.

reviewer_b.justification: The experimental evaluation is strong in breadth: comparisons to multiple AMR baselines and a high-precision grammar-based ERG parser, use of relevant metrics (EDM, Smatch, predicate/argument F1), and a runtime speed analysis that supports the efficiency claim. The paper reports hyperparameters and examines attention variants (hard pointer vs soft attention), but the summary does not clearly indicate a systematic ablation suite isolating all major design choices (e.g., stack features, joint predicate/alignment prediction), so sensitivity analysis is only partial. Related work coverage is reported as thorough, and several concrete limitations are acknowledged (concept prediction weakness for AMR, evaluation restricted to ERG-analysable sentences, potential lossy top-down linearization). Overall the evidence is substantial and well-documented but falls short of exhaustive ablations and full reproducibility (code not mentioned), so a solid but not perfect score is warranted.

main_claims: A neural encoder-decoder transition-based parser is developed as the first full-coverage semantic graph parser for Minimal Recursion Semantics (MRS).

The proposed parser (using stack-based embedding features and joint prediction of unlexicalized predicates and token alignments) is more accurate than attention-based baselines on MRS and also outperforms existing neural baselines on an AMR benchmark.

GPU batch processing makes the parser an order of magnitude faster than a high-precision grammar-based parser, and the MRS parser achieves an 86.69% Smatch score, argued to make MRS an attractive semantic representation.
_summary.methodology: A transition-based encoder-decoder neural parser (including arc-eager and top-down linearizations) that uses stack-based embedding features, pointer-network-based hard attention for alignments (and optional soft attention), predicting graphs jointly with unlexicalized predicates and token alignments.
baselines: Sequence-to-sequence AMR parsers (Barzdins and Gosko, 2016; Peng et al., 2017)
Synchronous Hyperedge Replacement Grammar (Peng and Gildea, 2016)
limitations: Concept prediction remains a weakness of the model for AMR.
Parsing/evaluation is limited to sentences for which the ERG has an acceptable analysis (approximately 15% of the original corpus cannot be evaluated).
The top-down linearization can be potentially lossy for reentrancies and relies on heuristic recovery of repeated dependent node information.
_summary.topic_area: NLP - semantic graph parsing (MRS/DMRS/EDS and AMR)

reviewer_a.name: Reproducible Systems Engineer
reviewer_a.background: ML/NLP engineer with a PhD in computational linguistics and 8+ years building and evaluating neural parsing systems; experience releasing production-ready parsers and reproducible research artifacts.
traits: insists on clear experimental protocols, hyperparameter settings, and training/validation/test splits to enable reproducibility
looks for strong, well-implemented baselines and comprehensive ablations that isolate the contribution of each model component
checks for appropriate statistical evaluation (e.g., multiple runs, variance reporting) and robust runtime/memory measurements
values code and data release, model sizes, and implementation details (tokenization, pretraining, optimizer) that affect performance and comparability
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Focused on empirical rigor and reproducibility: I evaluate whether the experiments convincingly support the claims and whether others can repeat and build on the work.

reviewer_b.name: Semantic Parsing Researcher
reviewer_b.background: Senior academic researcher with a track record in semantic representations (MRS, AMR, DMRS) and parsing; contributes to shared tasks and standards for meaning representations and studies their linguistic/engineering impact.
traits: assesses novelty and placement within prior work on semantic graph formalisms, parsing paradigms, and grammars
evaluates the practical and scientific significance of results for downstream applications and for advancing representation choices
notices limitations in coverage, linguistic adequacy (e.g., handling of scope, quantification, named entities), and whether claimed 'full coverage' is substantiated
values clear discussion of trade-offs between precision, coverage, and efficiency, and the implications for the field
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Focused on domain relevance and impact: I judge whether the paper meaningfully advances semantic parsing research and whether its claims and evaluation reflect real gains for the community.
